name: REPLACE: Descriptive name for this public opinion simulation
description: REPLACE: 2-3 sentence description of the event, announcement, or crisis being simulated and the organisation at its centre.

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of the organisation at the centre of public opinion
actors.description: REPLACE: Brief profile of the organisation — its size, sector, market position, and why it is at the centre of this opinion event.
objectives: Protect and restore brand reputation
Maintain customer and investor confidence
REPLACE: Add organisation-specific strategic objective
Minimise regulatory or legal consequences
constraints: REPLACE: Key constraint 1 (e.g., prior controversies, leadership credibility)
REPLACE: Key constraint 2 (e.g., financial exposure, contractual obligations)
Response speed limited by internal approval processes
available_actions: Issue public statement or press release
Hold press conference with CEO/spokesperson
Launch social media counter-narrative campaign
Engage crisis communications firm
Offer remediation or compensation to affected parties
Lobby regulator privately for favourable framing
Commission independent review or audit
Remain silent and wait for news cycle to move on

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of primary print/online news outlet (e.g., 'The National Gazette')
actors.description: REPLACE: The dominant print/online news outlet covering this sector. Readership profile, editorial lean, and relationship with protagonist.
objectives: Break news and maintain competitive advantage over rival outlets
Drive reader engagement and subscription growth
Maintain journalistic credibility and editorial independence
Win industry awards for investigative reporting
constraints: Must verify claims before publication (editorial standards)
Advertising revenue partially dependent on corporate sector
Small newsroom — limited investigative capacity
Defamation risk if reporting is inaccurate
available_actions: Publish breaking news story on the event
Assign investigative team for deeper reporting
Run opinion/editorial pieces framing the narrative
Interview affected parties and publish human interest stories
Platform expert commentary and analysis
Follow up with FOI requests or leaked documents
Syndicate story to international outlets for wider reach
Moderate comments section that shapes public perception

actors.name: Social Media Public
actors.description: The aggregate social media conversation across platforms (X/Twitter, Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, Reddit). Fragmented, fast-moving, and emotion-driven. Includes influencers, activists, and ordinary citizens sharing opinions, memes, and viral content about the event.
objectives: Express outrage or support (emotional catharsis)
Signal virtue or group identity through public positions
Drive viral content and gain followers/engagement
Hold powerful actors accountable through public pressure
constraints: Attention span is extremely short — 24-48 hour peak window
Vulnerable to misinformation and manipulation
No coordinated leadership — emergent behaviour only
Platform algorithms determine what goes viral
available_actions: Share news articles and add commentary
Create and spread memes satirising the protagonist
Launch hashtag campaigns (boycott, support, etc.)
Dox or target individuals associated with the event
Organise real-world protests via social coordination
Amplify or suppress competing narratives
Shift attention to next news cycle (natural decay)
Platform personal stories from affected individuals

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of primary public segment (e.g., 'Urban Professional Consumers')
actors.description: REPLACE: Description of the primary public segment — demographics, relationship to the protagonist's products/services, and baseline opinion.
objectives: Protect personal interests (health, financial, social)
Align behaviour with perceived social consensus
Punish or reward the protagonist based on perceived fairness
REPLACE: Segment-specific objective
constraints: Limited direct information — rely on media and social signals
Switching costs may prevent boycott even if sentiment is negative
Individual action feels powerless without collective mobilisation
Cognitive biases (confirmation bias, recency bias) shape interpretation
available_actions: Continue purchasing protagonist's products/services (status quo)
Reduce or stop purchasing (soft boycott)
Actively boycott and encourage others to join
Publicly defend the protagonist (counter-movement)
Sign petitions or join advocacy campaigns
Engage on social media to express opinion
Ignore the event entirely (apathy)
Switch to competitor products/services

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of secondary public segment (e.g., 'Rural Community Stakeholders')
actors.description: REPLACE: Description of the secondary public segment with a different opinion profile from segment A — perhaps more sympathetic, more hostile, or more indifferent.
objectives: REPLACE: Primary objective specific to this segment
REPLACE: Secondary objective specific to this segment
Seek accountability from the protagonist
Protect community or group identity
constraints: REPLACE: Segment-specific constraint
Less access to mainstream media — rely on social and community networks
May have stronger emotional connection to the event
Geographic or socioeconomic barriers to collective action
available_actions: Organise community meetings and collective response
Engage local political representatives
Provide testimony or evidence to media/regulators
Join or form advocacy coalitions
Take legal action (class action, individual claims)
Accept remediation offers from the protagonist
Disengage and focus on personal coping
Escalate to national or international attention

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of the relevant regulatory body (e.g., 'Consumer Protection Commission')
actors.description: REPLACE: The regulatory body with jurisdiction over the protagonist. Its mandate, enforcement powers, and political independence.
objectives: Fulfil statutory mandate to protect public interest
Demonstrate responsive but measured regulatory action
Avoid being seen as captured by the industry or as overreacting
Set precedent for future similar events
constraints: Enforcement powers may be limited by current legislation
Investigation takes time — cannot match media cycle speed
Political pressure from both industry and public sides
Resource constraints limit simultaneous enforcement actions
available_actions: Issue statement acknowledging awareness of the event
Open formal investigation or inquiry
Issue preliminary findings or warning letters
Impose interim measures (cease and desist, temporary suspension)
Hold public hearings to gather stakeholder input
Coordinate with other regulatory bodies or law enforcement
Impose fines or sanctions based on investigation findings
Propose new regulations to prevent recurrence

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of relevant advocacy or pressure group (e.g., 'Consumer Rights Alliance')
actors.description: REPLACE: An advocacy group, NGO, or pressure organisation that opposes the protagonist's position. Its credibility, funding, and mobilisation capacity.
objectives: Hold the protagonist accountable for the event
Achieve concrete remediation or policy change
Grow membership and funding through high-profile campaign
Shift broader public opinion toward their cause
constraints: Limited budget and staff for sustained campaign
Credibility depends on factual accuracy — vulnerable to discrediting
Must balance confrontation with constructive engagement
Donor expectations may push for visible wins over long-term change
available_actions: Launch public awareness campaign against the protagonist
File formal complaints with regulators
Organise protests, petitions, or boycott campaigns
Engage legal counsel for potential class action
Brief media with research, data, and affected individuals
Negotiate privately with protagonist for settlement
Coalition-build with other advocacy groups for amplified pressure
Publish independent research or counter-narrative reports

actors.name: REPLACE: Name of primary competitor (e.g., 'GlobalTech Industries')
actors.description: REPLACE: The protagonist's primary competitor. How they might exploit this event — through marketing, poaching customers, or public statements of differentiation.
objectives: Capture market share from the protagonist during reputation damage
Position brand as the responsible alternative
Avoid own exposure to similar criticism (glass house risk)
Strengthen relationships with protagonist's dissatisfied customers
constraints: Overt exploitation may backfire as opportunistic
May have similar vulnerabilities that could be exposed in retaliation
Industry solidarity norms may discourage public criticism
Must balance short-term gains against long-term competitor relationship
available_actions: Remain publicly silent but increase targeted marketing
Launch competitive offers targeting protagonist's customers
Issue subtle comparative messaging (without naming protagonist)
Publicly differentiate on the specific issue (e.g., safety, ethics)
Poach protagonist's disaffected employees
Brief analysts on own company's superior practices
Support industry-wide standards that implicitly criticise protagonist
Do nothing — avoid drawing attention to the sector-wide issue

relationships.description: Media intensely covering the protagonist; relationship ranges from adversarial to cooperative depending on transparency
Print stories are shared and amplified on social media, often with added commentary
Social media shapes public segment A's perception through viral content and peer signals
Social media influences public segment B, though community networks also play a role
Traditional media is a primary information source for this public segment
Regulatory authority with enforcement powers over the protagonist
Pressure group actively challenging the protagonist's conduct
Advocacy group pushing regulator to take action
Advocacy group provides media with research, data, and affected individuals for stories
Customer segment whose purchasing decisions directly affect the protagonist
Community stakeholder segment affected by the protagonist's actions
Market competitor monitoring the protagonist for exploitable weakness
Competitor targeting the same customer segment
Regulator's public statements are reported by media, shaping the narrative

prediction_questions: Will public sentiment shift positive or negative within the first 20 rounds?
Which media channel will be the first to break a damaging story?
Will the regulator open a formal investigation before round 40?
Will the competitor successfully capture market share from the protagonist?
Will the advocacy group's campaign gain mainstream traction?
Will the protagonist's initial response strategy succeed or require revision?
Will social media sentiment peak negative within the first 10 rounds or build slowly?
Will any actor form an unexpected alliance?
